elements.sent-2411-001: Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) is a crucial tool for clinical diagnosis while facing the challenge of long scanning time.
elements.sent-2411-002: To reduce the acquisition time, fast MRI reconstruction aims to restore high-quality images from the undersampled k-space.
elements.sent-2411-003: Existing methods typically train deep learning models to map the undersampled data to artifact-free MRI images.
elements.sent-2411-004: However, these studies often overlook the unique properties of k-space and directly apply general networks designed for image processing to k-space recovery, leaving the precise learning of k-space largely underexplored.
elements.sent-2411-005: In this work, we propose a continuous k-space recovery network from a new perspective of implicit neural representation with image domain guidance, which boosts the performance of MRI reconstruction.
elements.sent-2411-006: Specifically, (1) an implicit neural representation based encoder-decoder structure is customized to continuously query unsampled k-values.
elements.sent-2411-007: Extensive experiments conducted on CC359, fastMRI, and IXI datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of our method and its superiority over other competitors.
elements.sent-2411-008: However, the MRI acquisition process is inherently slow due to both physiological and hardware limitations, as well as the sequential nature of obtaining measurements in the frequency domain (k-space)~\cite{r1,r2 .}
elements.sent-2411-009: The extended scan time required to obtain fully sampled MRI images causes patient discomfort and reduces accessibility to the modality.
elements.sent-2411-010: To speed up this process, accelerated MRI aims to reconstruct MRI images from undersampled k-space~\cite{r3,r4,r5 .}
elements.sent-2411-011: However, the aliasing artifacts caused by such insufficient sampling often affect the clinical diagnosis.
elements.sent-2411-012: Therefore, it becomes a significant challenge how to reduce the amount of k-space acquisition while maintaining or even improving MRI image quality.
elements.sent-2411-013: Essentially, the aliasing artifacts arise because undersampling destroys the k-space’s integrity.
elements.sent-2411-014: Many methods ignore the importance of k-space recovery and focus on reconstruction only from low-quality MRI images~\cite{r6,r7,r8,r9,r10 .}
elements.sent-2411-015: To this end, several works consider recovering k-space via an external network as shown in Fig.\ref{Fig.1 (a)~\cite{r11,r12,r13,r14,r15,r16,r17,9393325 .}}
elements.sent-2411-016: Despite some progress, they neglect the unique properties inherent to the k-space and directly adopt general CNNs which are not suitable for k-space recovery.
elements.sent-2411-017: CNNs have an inductive bias that their kernels are shared across spatial positions.
elements.sent-2411-018: However, in k-space, spatial positions represent frequency components of sine and cosine functions, and identical patterns at different positions can represent entirely different information\cite{r19,r20,r24,r25 .}
elements.sent-2411-019: Previous works overlook this specific characteristic of k-space and adopt conventional image processing networks for processing k-space, resulting in inferior results.
elements.sent-2411-020: Hence, designing a customized network for undersampled k-space recovery is the key to reconstructing satisfactory MRI images.
elements.figure-2411-001: \centerline{\includegraphics[width=1.0\linewidth]{Figs/Fig1.pdf}} \caption{Our main idea. (a) Previous studies typically employ general networks to reconstruct the undersampled data both in the k-space and image domain. (b) We customize a continuous k-space recovery network from a new perspective of implicit neural representation with image domain guidance, thereby enhancing the performance of MRI reconstruction.} \label{Fig.1}
elements.sent-2411-021: Intuitively, a high-quality MRI image is generated from a continuous k-space where each coordinate has a specific signal.
elements.sent-2411-022: Therefore, the ideal reconstruction of k-space should be modeled as a function where arbitrary coordinates can be mapped into a k-value.
elements.sent-2411-023: Such a concept of continuous representation is consistent with implicit neural representation (INR), which emerged as a new paradigm for image super-resolution~\cite{r21,r22 .}
elements.sent-2411-024: The network takes spatial coordinates as input and predicts corresponding pixel values.
elements.sent-2411-025: After training on low-resolution data, the model generates high-resolution images by querying dense coordinates, preserving fine details and enabling arbitrary resolution output~\cite{r21,r23 .}
elements.sent-2411-026: Inspired by this, we propose to boost the performance of MRI reconstruction by recovering complete k-space via implicit neural representation.
elements.sent-2411-027: However, simply introducing INR to MRI reconstruction still faces several challenges.
elements.sent-2411-028: Compared to it, the k-space spectrum exhibits a simple structure, making such continuous recovery challenging.
elements.sent-2411-029: Therefore, more information needed to be provided to guide k-space reconstruction.
elements.sent-2411-030: A more gentle recovery paradigm should be designed to progressively reconstruct the dense k-space.
elements.sent-2411-031: Based on these, we propose an Image-domain Guided K-space Recovery Network, named IGKR-Net.
elements.sent-2411-032: As shown in Fig.\ref{Fig.1 (b), we customize a k-space network from a new perspective of INR with image guidance for MRI reconstruction.}
elements.sent-2411-033: Specifically, (1) We propose an INR-based encoder-decoder structure, directly processing signals in the k-space domain.
elements.sent-2411-034: To detail, we design an encoder based on the standard transformer including multi-head self-attention (MSA) and feed-forward network (FFN), to encode the sampled k-values and their corresponding coordinates.
elements.sent-2411-035: Thus, a continuous feature representation of the k-space spectrum is learned.
elements.sent-2411-036: Subsequently, we adopt standard transformer decoder layers consisting of multi-head cross-attention (MCA), MSA, and FFN, to dynamically query the unsampled k-values using their coordinates and thus reconstruct the k-space spectrum with high quality.
elements.sent-2411-037: Overall, the main contributions of our work are as follows: • An INR based encoder-decoder structure is proposed to directly process signals in the k-space.
elements.sent-2411-038: Benefiting from this structure, our work can reconstruct the high-quality k-space.
elements.sent-2411-039: Thanks to the design of this, we provide rich semantic information for the challenging k-space recovery.
elements.sent-2411-040: Thanks to this paradigm, we mitigate concerns of over-smoothing and distortion and obtain precise results.
elements.sent-2411-041: Traditional MRI techniques involve the acquisition of dense k-space data, resulting in extended scan time.
elements.sent-2411-042: In recent years, a variety of deep neural networks have been explored for accelerated MRI.
elements.sent-2411-043: The pipeline for these methods usually consists of three steps, i.e., roughly recovering the complete k-space from undersampled data by employing a zero-filling strategy, then generating low-quality MRI images via inverse fast Fourier transform (IFFT), and finally learning the mapping relationship between such low-quality MRI images and their corresponding high-quality ones by adapting advanced CNN architectures, including UNet~\cite{r28 , deep residual network~\cite{r29 , generative adversarial network~\cite{r30 , and deep cascaded network~\cite{r31 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-044: Besides, benefiting from the capability of learning global information, transformers~\cite{Yang_2024_CVPR,yang2024tacklingambiguityperspectiveuncertainty,10196308,10155416 have recently achieved satisfactory performance for accelerated MRI.}
elements.sent-2411-045: Feng et al.~\cite{r32,r33 pioneered the introduction of the transformer in the field of MRI reconstruction, integrating MRI reconstruction with super-resolution in a multitask fashion.}
elements.sent-2411-046: Huang et al.~\cite{r34 proposed a parallel imaging coupled swin transformer-based model.}
elements.sent-2411-047: To sum up, the data-driven nature of deep learning allows these methods to learn the mapping between low-quality input and artifact-free images.
elements.sent-2411-048: However, such networks operate only in the image domain, making it challenging to ensure the consistency of the k-space.
elements.sent-2411-049: To this end, several attempts have been made to incorporate physics information in this line of work, including enforcing k-space consistency directly after image enhancement or adding k-space consistency as an additional cost function term during training.
elements.sent-2411-050: For example, Hyun et al.~\cite{r11 enhanced k-space consistency by directly replacing the generated k-values with the corresponding original ones.}
elements.sent-2411-051: Yang et al.~\cite{r12 considered incorporating an additional k-space consistency term into the loss function.}
elements.sent-2411-052: Though effective at reducing artifacts, they fundamentally only focus on image-domain restoration, with k-space information used for coarse correction or consistency supervision.
elements.sent-2411-053: The images relied upon by such methods are severely compromised, and this damage is irreversible~\cite{r26,r27 .}
elements.sent-2411-054: Indeed, the continuity of the k-space is crucial for the recovery of high-quality MRI images.
elements.sent-2411-055: However, these approaches neglect this and result in inferior results.
elements.sent-2411-056: Recently, some studies began to recover k-space.
elements.sent-2411-057: Taejoon Eo et al.~\cite{r35 proposed KIKINet, using a combination of 4 different CNNs to operate on k-space, image, k-space, and image sequentially.}
elements.sent-2411-058: Osvald Nitski et al.~\cite{r36 proposed CDF-Net, using two UNet to operate on the image and k-space domain respectively, and combining them into an end-to-end framework.}
elements.sent-2411-059: Wang et al.~\cite{10606502 introduced a compressed sensing equivariant imaging prior framework that combines a data preparation strategy for generalizable MRI reconstruction.}
elements.sent-2411-060: Zhao et al.~\cite{r38 proposed SwinGAN, consisting of a k-space generator and an image-domain generator that utilizes swin transformer as the backbone.}
elements.sent-2411-061: Such approaches aimed to preserve and leverage the k-space information, achieving more accurate results.
elements.sent-2411-062: However, they all treated the k-space spectrums as natural images, directly employing general networks for feature extraction and spectrum reconstruction.
elements.sent-2411-063: Besides, in k-space based parallel imaging reconstruction, some methods such as GRAPPA~\cite{r56 , SPIRiT~\cite{r57 and AC-LORAKS~\cite{r58 used linear convolution to interpolate undersampled k-space data.}}}
elements.sent-2411-064: They assumed that the k-space data has a shifted autoregressive structure and learned the scan-specific autoregressive relationships required to recover missing sample scans from the fully sampled auto-calibration (ACS) data.
elements.sent-2411-065: However, at high acceleration, these methods produce inherent noise amplification and degrade image quality.
elements.sent-2411-066: Recently, the structure of the linear GRAPPA method has been transformed into a nonlinear deep learning method called RAKI~\cite{r59 .}
elements.sent-2411-067: RAKI performs scan-specific k-space interpolation using CNNs trained on the ACS data, and provides an improvement in both image quality and noise reduction over traditional PI methods that use linear k-space interpolation.
elements.sent-2411-068: However, the availability of training databases with different scans and their interpretability hinders its adaptability.
elements.figure-2411-002: \centerline{\includegraphics[width=1.0\linewidth]{Figs/Fig2.pdf}} \caption{The overall architecture of the proposed IGKR Net. Given the undersampled k-space, we first encode the coordinates and k-values of the sampled points, and the resulting features are added and then fed into LRIT. In LRIT, we use the LR coordinates to query and get the recovered results, i.e., $\widehat{{K}}_{1}$ and $\widehat{{I}}_{1}$. Next, we send $\widehat{{I}}_{1}$ into IDGM and get $\widehat{{K}}_{2}$ and $\widehat{{I}}_{2}$ with the guidance of the low-quality image $I_{s}$. Then, the k-values and coordinates of $\widehat{{K}}_{2}$ are encoded and fed into HRIT, where $\widehat{{K}}_{3}$ and $\widehat{{I}}_{3}$ are obtained by querying HR coordinates. Finally, we design TARM to refine $\widehat{{I}}_{3}$, yielding the final output $\widehat{{K}}_{4}$ and $\widehat{{I}}_{4}$.} \label{Fig.2}
elements.sent-2411-069: Recently, implicit neural representation (INR) has been proposed as a method of continuous representation for various tasks~\cite{r39,r40,r41,r42,r43,r44 .}
elements.sent-2411-070: INR employs neural networks, typically coordinate-based multilayer merceptrons (MLPs), to establish mapping relationships between coordinates and their corresponding signal values~\cite{10621671,10261266 .}
elements.sent-2411-071: Because of its continuous and precise representation, INR has been applied to various tasks of image enhancement~\cite{r21,r22 .}
elements.sent-2411-072: For example, LIIF~\cite{r21 proposed a new framework for arbitrary-scale super-resolution using INR.}
elements.sent-2411-073: Li et al.~\cite{r23 proposed a novel adaptive local image function (A-LIIF) to alleviate structural distortions and ringing artifacts around edges.}
elements.sent-2411-074: Motivated by the feature interpolation in LIIF, Wu et al.~\cite{r51 designed an INR-based network for achieving 3D MRI super-resolution at arbitrary scales.}
elements.sent-2411-075: In summary, 2D INR models an image as a continuous function using latent space interpolation, enabling precise querying of signal intensity for any given coordinates.
elements.sent-2411-076: This concept provides a novel approach for achieving high-quality recovery in k-space.
elements.sent-2411-077: Let $K$ represent the complex-valued, fully sampled k-space acquired from the MRI scanner, accelerated MRI usually employs undersampling to acquire a reduced set of k-space, i.e., $K_{s}$, while a substantial portion of the k-space, i.e., $K_{us}$, is unsampled.
elements.sent-2411-078: Here, we simulate this undersampling process by the element-wise multiplication ($\otimes$) of $K$ with a two-dimensional mask $M$: \begin{equation}K_{s}=M\otimes K,K_{us}=(1-M)\otimes K\label{eq1}\end{equation} Correspondingly, low-quality MRI image $I_{s}$ can be obtained, i.e., $I_{s = IFFT(K_{s )$.}}
elements.sent-2411-079: To recover high-quality MRI image $\widehat{{I }$, deep learning methods typically leverage extensive training data to establish a mapping relationship between $\widehat{{I }$ and the sampled data $(I_{s , K_{s )$, which can be formulated as: \begin{equation}\widehat{{I}}=f_{\theta}(I_{s},K_{s})\label{eq2}\end{equation} Here, $\theta$ is the parameters set of the deep neural network.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-080: In contrast to these methods, our IGKR-Net focuses on recovering $K_{us}$, which is the fundamental cause of MRI image blurring and is crucial for high-quality MRI reconstruction.
elements.figure-2411-003: \centerline{\includegraphics[width=1.0\linewidth]{Figs/Fig3.pdf}} \caption{(a) The proposed IDGM consists of two stages, i.e., shallow fusion stage and deep fusion stage. (b) The proposed TARM consists of three branches, i.e., PA, CA, and SA.} \label{Fig.3}
elements.sent-2411-081: In contrast to the typical representation of images using discrete pixel arrays, implicit neural representation (INR) models the mapping relationship between image coordinates $C$ and their corresponding intensity values $i$ as a function, which can be formulated as: \begin{equation}i = f_{\theta}(C,V_{c})\label{eq3}\end{equation} Here, $V_{c}$ represents the feature vector specific to the pixel at spatial coordinates $C$ and is obtained by establishing a latent space by training an encoder.
elements.sent-2411-082: Function $f_{\theta}$ is fitted using a multilayer perceptron (MLP) and is continuous to the spatial coordinate system.
elements.sent-2411-083: Therefore, once $f_{\theta}$ is well-fitted, intensity values can be queried at arbitrary coordinates, thereby achieving a continuous visual representation of the image.
elements.subsubsection-2411-003: continuous K-space Recovery Network via INR
elements.sent-2411-084: As described in \eqref{eq1 , it is common to undersample the k-space for accelerated MRI.}
elements.sent-2411-085: The correct recovery of the unsampled k-values, i.e., $K_{us}$, is crucial for high-quality MRI reconstruction.
elements.sent-2411-086: Drawing inspiration from the continuous image modeling of INR, we customize a k-space recovery net.
elements.sent-2411-087: Specifically, $K_{us}$ can be queried by using their coordinates $C_{us}$: \begin{equation}K_{{us}}=f_{\theta}(C_{{us}},V_{C_{us}}) \label{eq4}\end{equation} Where the latent feature vectors $V_{C_{us }$ are obtained by encoding the sampled k-values $K_{s}$ and their coordinates $C_{s}$ using a designed encoder $\Phi_{Enc}$.}
elements.sent-2411-088: The function $f_{\theta}$ is fitted through training a decoder $\Phi_{Dec}$, which can be formulated as: \begin{equation}K_{{us}}=\Phi_{Dec}(C_{{us}},\Phi_{Enc}(K_{s},C_{s}))\label{eq5}\end{equation} Here, the encoder $\Phi_{Enc}$ and decoder $\Phi_{Dec}$ are implemented through the transformer attention mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-089: As shown in Fig.\ref{Fig.2 , the overall architecture of our IGKR-Net can be roughly divided into two parts, i.e., the primary part directly processing in k-space domain (denoted as \textcolor{red {red lines ), and the auxiliary part operating on the data of image domain (denoted as \textcolor{blue {blue lines ).}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-090: The k-space part comprises two modules, i.e., LRIT and HRIT, to reconstruct the k-space from low resolution (LR) to high resolution (HR) progressively, while the image part comprises IDGM and TRAM for image-domain guidance and final refinement, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-091: Specifically, given undersampled k-space $K \in R^{2\times H \times W}$ as input, we obtain the set of k-values $K_{s}$ and the corresponding coordinates $C_{s}$, from the sampled points.
elements.sent-2411-092: Then, $K_{s}$ and $C_{s}$ are encoded separately, and the resulting features are summed to form a vector sequence which is used as the input of LRIT.
elements.sent-2411-093: LRIT first uses a transformer-based encoder to learn a latent space and then recover a complete while LR k-space $\widehat{{K _{1}$ by querying pre-designed LR coordinates through the decoder.}}
elements.sent-2411-094: Subsequently, we get $\widehat{{I _{1}$ through IFFT and send it into IDGM to deeply mine semantic information from the corrupted image $I_{s}$ and guide the k-space recovery.}}
elements.sent-2411-096: Next, HRIT uses the recovered dense $\widehat{{K _{2}$ to further establish a continuous latent space, recovering complete and HR $\widehat{K _{3}$ and its corresponding $\widehat{{I _{3}$ using the HR coordinates.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-097: Finally, we design TARM to refine $\widehat{{I _{3}$ in the image domain, resulting in the final output, i.e., $\widehat{{I _{4}$ and $\widehat{{K _{4}$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-098: Besides, to mitigate issues of over-smoothing and distortion and obtain satisfactory results progressively, a multi-stage training strategy is proposed.
elements.sent-2411-099: Below we first present the design of LRIT, IDGM, HRIT, and TARM, respectively, followed by a description of the training strategy.
elements.sent-2411-100: LRIT and HRIT share the same structure but differ in their inputs.
elements.sent-2411-101: Here, we take LRIT as an example to explain its structure in detail.
elements.sent-2411-102: As depicted in Fig.\ref{Fig.2 (a), LRIT is an encoder-decoder architecture aiming to recover LR k-space $\widehat{{K _{1}$ and its corresponding $\widehat{{I _{1}$ by querying the LR coordinates.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-103: Specifically, given a set of sampled k-values $K_{s}$ and its coordinates $C_{s}$, a tokenization procedure is firstly applied to convert them into a vector sequence $\mathcal{V}$: \begin{equation}\mathcal{V} = MLP(K_{s})+PE(C_{s}) \label{eq6}\end{equation} where a multilayer perceptrons (MLPs) layer is applied to $K_{s}$, and PE refers to positional encodings with sine and cosine functions.
elements.sent-2411-104: Then we send $\mathcal{V}$ into the encoder of LRIT which consists of multi-head self-attention (MSA) and feed-forward network (FFN) to obtain the latent space $\mathcal{S}$ as follows: \begin{equation}\mathcal{S} = FFN(MSA(\mathcal{V})) \label{eq7}\end{equation} Subsequently, we design a decoder that employs standard transformer decoder layers consisting of multi-head cross attention (MCA), MSA, and FFN, to recover complete while low-resolution k-space, i.e., $\widehat{{K _{1}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-105: In detail, for MCA, we employ encoded LR coordinates ${C _{lr \in R^{\frac{H {2 \times \frac{W {2 }$ to serve as the query ($Q$) and apply two different linear projects to latent space $\mathcal{S}$ to serve as the key ($K$) and value ($V$).}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-106: After that, we apply MSA to the weighted feature again and decode it via FFN.
elements.sent-2411-107: Thus, we obtain a low-resolution while complete k-space $\widehat{{K _{1}\in R^{2\times \frac{H {2 \times \frac{W {2 }$, as well as its corresponding MRI image $\widehat{{I _{1}\in R^{1\times \frac{H {2 \times \frac{W {2 }$.}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-109: As shown in Fig.\ref{Fig.2 (b), our IDGM fuses the low-quality image $I_{s}$ and the output of LRIT, i.e., $\widehat{{I _{1}$, to fully utilize the original image information and guide the process of k-space recovery.}}}
elements.sent-2411-110: Fig.\ref{Fig.3 (a) illustrates the detailed structure of the IDGM, which can be divided into two stages: shallow stage and deep stage.}
elements.sent-2411-111: Specifically, during the shallow stage, we firstly perform two convolutions with different strides to extract shallow features from $I_{s}$ and $\widehat{{I _{1}$ and unify them to the same size, i.e., ${\widehat {I _{1 \in R^{1\times \frac{H {2 \times \frac{W {2 }\to R^{h\times \frac{H {2 \times \frac{W {2 }$, ${I_{s \in R^{1\times H \times W }\to R^{h\times \frac{H {2 \times \frac{W {2 }$, where h denotes the channel number.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-112: Subsequently, the features are fused via element-wise summation and restored to ${{I _{up \in R^{1\times H \times W }$ via another convolution and upsampling $up(\cdot)$, i.e., $I_{up = up(conv(conv({\widehat {{I _{1 )+conv(I_{s )))$.}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-113: During the deep fusion, we first employ two convolution heads to extract image-specific shallow features from $I_{s}$ and ${{I _{up}$, respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-114: After element-wise summation, the fused feature is sent into two attention branches, generating local channel attention $A_{l}$ without any pooling and global channel attention $A_{g}$ by using global average pooling, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-115: Given $A_{l}$ and $A_{g}$, the final attention weights $A$ can be obtained by the broadcasted addition ($+$), i.e., $A= sigmoid(A_{l + A_{g )$.}}
elements.sent-2411-116: Finally, we obtain $\widehat{{I _{2}$, as well as its corresponding k-space $\widehat{{K _{2}$: \begin{equation}\widehat {{I}}_{2} = conv(A\otimes conv({I}_{up})) \label{eq10}\end{equation} \begin{equation}\widehat{{K}}_{2}={FFT}(\widehat{{I}}_{2}) \label{eq11}\end{equation}.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-117: As shown in Fig.\ref{Fig.2 (c), to further reconstruct the dense k-space with high resolution, we apply the implicit transformer again.}
elements.sent-2411-118: Different from the above LRIT, here we use the $K_{s '$ and $C_{s '$ from restored $\widehat{{K _{2}$ to form vector sequence $\mathcal{V'}$, i.e., $\mathcal{V' = MLP(K_{s ')+PE(C_{s ')$.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-119: Then we get latent space $\mathcal{S'}$: \begin{equation}\mathcal{S'} = FFN(MSA(\mathcal{V'})) \label{eq12}\end{equation} Finally, we obtain the high quality k-space $\widehat{{K _{3}$ and its corresponding MRI image $\widehat{{I _{3}$ by encoding HR coordinates $C_{hr}\in R^{ H \times W }$ as query ($Q'$) and projecting $\mathcal{S'}$ as key ($K'$) and value ($V'$).}}}}

elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{Training procedure} \LinesNumbered {$stage\_1$: Train LRIT}\; \For{ each $epoch\in \left [ E_{0},E_{1}\right]$ } { Get $({{K_{s}}},{{C_{s}}},{C_{lr}},{K_{lr}},{I_{lr}})$; Train LRIT by minimizing $L_{stage\_1} = L_{1} $\ } {$stage\_2$: Joint optimization of LRIT and DIFM}\; \For{ each $epoch\in \left [ E_{1},E_{2}\right]$ } { Get $({{K_{s}}},{{C_{s}}},{C_{lr}},{K_{lr}},{I_{lr}},{I_{s}},{K},{I})$; Train LRIT and DIFM by minimizing $L_{stage\_2} = L_{1} + L_{2} $\ } {$stage\_3$: Joint optimization of LRIT, DIFM, and HRIT}\; \For{ each $epoch\in \left [ E_{2},E_{3}\right]$ } { Get $({{K_{s}}},{{C_{s}}},{C_{lr}},{K_{lr}},{I_{lr}},{I_{s}},{K},{I},{C_{us}})$; Train LRIT, DIFM, and HRIT by minimizing $L_{stage\_3} = L_{1} + L_{2} + L_{3} $\ } {$stage\_4$: Joint optimization of LRIT, DIFM, HRIT, and TARM}\; \For{ each $epoch\in \left [ E_{3},E_{4}\right]$ } { Get $({{K_{s}}},{{C_{s}}},{C_{lr}},{K_{lr}},{I_{lr}},{I_{s}},{K},{I},{C_{us}})$; Train LRIT, DIFM, HRIT, TARM by minimizing $L_{stage\_4} = L_{1} + L_{2} + L_{3} + L_{4}$\ } \label{A1}
elements.sent-2411-121: As shown in Fig.\ref{Fig.2 (d), our TARM further refines the reconstruction result $\widehat{{I _{3}$ in the image domain, providing it with richer image details.}}}
elements.sent-2411-122: Fig.\ref{Fig.3 (b) illustrates the detailed structure of the TARM, which comprises a convolution head and three attention branches, i.e., spatial attention (SA), channel attention (CA), and pixel attention (PA).}
elements.sent-2411-123: Specifically, for an image that needs to be refined, i.e., $\widehat{{I _{3}$, the TARM firstly extracts shallow feature $f$ by a convolution.}}
elements.sent-2411-124: Then, $f$ is sent into three attention branches.
elements.sent-2411-125: To detail, PA generates a 3D attention mask $M_{1}$ without any pooling or sampling, which means the output features have local information.
elements.sent-2411-126: CA uses global average pooling (GAP) to generate the 1D attention mask, i.e., $M_{2}$, to obtain features that have global information.
elements.sent-2411-127: SA uses GAP or max average pooling (MAP) to generate a 2D attention mask, i.e., $M_{3}$, to get features with global information.
elements.sent-2411-128: Finally, the weighted features from three attention branches are fused via element-wise summation, to obtain the final reconstruction results $\widehat{{I _{4}$ and its corresponding k-space $\widehat{{K _{4}$: \begin{equation}\widehat{{I}}_{4}= conv(M_{1}\otimes f +M_{2}\otimes f+M_{3}\otimes f) \label{eq15}\end{equation} \begin{equation}\widehat{{K}}_4={FFT}(\widehat{{I}}_4) \label{eq16}\end{equation}.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-129: Following \cite{r37,r38 , we evaluate the intermediate outputs of various modules by using the $L_{2}$ loss, which consists of two terms.}
elements.sent-2411-130: To detail, the first term is used to constrain the consistency between the reconstructed k-space $\widehat{\mathrm{K _{i}$ and corresponding fully sampled k-space, i.e., $K_{lr}$ or $K$, while the second term is used to constrain the consistency between the reconstructed image $\widehat{\mathrm{I _{i}$ and the corresponding fully sampled image, i.e., $I_{lr}$ or $I$: \begin{equation}L_{i}=\begin{cases} \left \| K_{lr}-\widehat{K}_{i}\right \| _{2} + \left \| I_{lr}-\widehat{I}_{i}\right \| _{2} \text{ if }i=1 [11pt] \left \| K_{\textcolor{white}{lr}} -\widehat{K}_{i}\right \| _{2} + \left \| I_{\textcolor{white}{lr}}-\widehat{I}_{i}\right \| _{2} \text{ if } i=2,3,4 \end{cases} \label{eq17}\end{equation} where $I$, $I_{lr}$, and $K_{lr}$ is obtained via $I=IFFT(K)$, $I_{lr =downsample(I)$, and $k_{lr =FFT(I_{lr )$, respsectively.}}}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-017: \begin{equation}L_{i}= \left \| K_{lr}-\widehat{K}_{i}\right \| _{2} + \left \| I_{lr}-\widehat{I}_{i}\right \| _{2}  if i=1 [11pt] \left \| K_{\textcolor{white}{lr}} -\widehat{K}_{i}\right \| _{2} + \left \| I_{\textcolor{white}{lr}}-\widehat{I}_{i}\right \| _{2}  if  i=2,3,4  \label{eq17}\end{equation}

elements.sent-2411-184: In this paper, we focused on the problem of image-domain guided k-space recovery for MRI reconstruction.
elements.sent-2411-185: We proposed a novel method, called IGKR-Net, which tackled the key problem of current deep learning based MRI reconstruction methods, namely the insufficient and unsuitable k-space recovery.
elements.sent-2411-186: Specifically, an implicit transformer based k-space reconstruction is proposed to efficiently learn continuous feature space and recover high-quality k-space in the Fourier domain.
elements.sent-2411-187: This mechanism facilitates more precise and effective learning of continuous representations of k-space information, resulting in precise k-space spectrum that are more favorable for MRI reconstruction.
elements.sent-2411-188: Furthermore, an image domain enhancement branch consists of a fusion module and a refinement module is introduced to enable accurate dual-domain feature aggregation and image refinement, thereby guiding MRI reconstruction.
elements.sent-2411-189: Our method demonstrates superior performance on publicly available datasets, confirming its effectiveness.. \bibliographystyle{IEEEtran .}

elements.sent-2411-239: Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) is widely used for clinical diagnosis because of its advantages of no radiation, high resolution, and satisfactory soft-tissue contrast.
elements.sent-2411-240: However, the acquisition of dense k-space for accurate MRI images inherently leads to long time due to both physiological and hardware limitations~\citep{r1,r2 .}
elements.sent-2411-241: The extended scan time causes patient discomfort and reduces accessibility to MRI.
elements.sent-2411-242: To speed up this process, accelerated MRI aims to reconstruct MRI images from undersampled k-space~\citep{r3,r4,r5 .}
elements.sent-2411-243: In recent years, deep learning has emerged as a powerful tool to tackle this problem.
elements.sent-2411-244: These methods usually learn the mapping between low-quality MRI images and their corresponding high-quality ones by adapting advanced neural network architectures~\citep{ReconFormer,FPS,r8,aghabiglou2024mr,velayudham2025improving .}
elements.sent-2411-245: In other words, the main purpose of this methodology is to achieve MRI reconstruction purely in the image domain.
elements.sent-2411-246: Although they have succeeded in removing artifacts in the image domain, k-space recovery cannot be achieved, resulting in unsatisfactory reconstruction results.
elements.sent-2411-247: Essentially, the aliasing artifacts arise because the accelerated undersampling destroys the k-space’s integrity.
elements.sent-2411-248: To this end, several works consider recovering k-space via an external network as shown in Figure.\ref{Fig.1 (a)~\citep{r35, r37,MD-Recon-Net,r38 .}}
elements.sent-2411-249: However, in k-space, spatial positions represent frequency components of sine and cosine functions, and identical patterns at different positions can represent entirely different information\citep{r20,r25 .}

Given the undersampled k-space, we first encode the coordinates $C_{s}$ and k-values $K_{s}$ of the sampled points, and the resulting features are added and then fed into LRIT. In LRIT, we use the LR coordinates to query and get the recovered results, i.e., $\widehat{{K}}_{1}$ and $\widehat{{I}}_{1}$. elements.sent-2411-282: Recently, implicit neural representation (INR) has been proposed as a method of continuous representation for various tasks~\citep{r39,r40,r41,r42,r43,r44 .}
elements.sent-2411-283: INR employs neural networks, typically coordinate-based multilayer merceptrons (MLPs), to establish mapping relationships between coordinates and their corresponding signal values~\citep{10621671,10261266,LI2025126487 .}
elements.sent-2411-284: Because of its continuous and precise representation, INR has been applied to various tasks of image enhancement~\citep{r21,r22 .}
elements.sent-2411-285: For example, LIIF~\citep{r21 proposed a new framework for arbitrary-scale super-resolution using INR.}
elements.sent-2411-286: Li et al.~\citep{r23 proposed a novel adaptive local image function (A-LIIF) to alleviate structural distortions and ringing artifacts around edges.}
elements.sent-2411-287: Motivated by the feature interpolation in LIIF, Wu et al.~\citep{r51 designed an INR-based network for 3D MRI super-resolution at arbitrary scales.}
elements.sent-2411-288: In summary, 2D INR models an image as a continuous function using latent space, enabling precise querying of signal intensity for any given coordinates.

elements.sent-2411-359: We can observe that: (a) When removing the low- to high-resolution progressive learning strategy, i.e., querying the unsampled k-values directly using high-resolution coordinates, the model produces a significant performance degradation from PSNR $33.06$ to $31.65$.
elements.sent-2411-360: Direct recovery of high-resolution k-space from overly undersampled data leads to oversmoothing, which results in poorer reconstruction results.
elements.sent-2411-361: This illustrates the effectiveness of our low- to high-resolution multi-stage learning strategy.
elements.sent-2411-362: This suggests that using the dense k-space obtained from LRIT to further construct a more accurate latent space, and querying the k-space by high-resolution coordinates is important for effective MRI reconstruction.

elements.sent-2411-365: SwinMR obtained a small number of parameters but with a large computational complexity (FLOPs=$398$G) and suboptimal reconstruction results.
elements.sent-2411-366: ReconFormer used a local pyramidal but global columnar transformer structure, which achieved a lightweight parameter of $1.14$M via its recurrent nature.
elements.sent-2411-367: However, the larger computational complexity (FLOPs = $342$G) hinders its training and testing process.
elements.sent-2411-368: Our IGKR-Net outperformed other methods and obtained an optimal balance between the number of parameters and computational complexity.
elements.sent-2411-369: This paper introduces IGKR-Net, a network that addresses the challenging problem of k-space recovery caused by under-sampling, using INR as a new perspective.
elements.sent-2411-370: Furthermore, by introducing an image guidance module that extracts information from low-quality MRI images and proposing a tri-attention refinement module that enhances the resulting image quality, IGKR-Net ultimately achieves accurate MRI reconstruction.
elements.sent-2411-371: Experimental results demonstrate our method's effectiveness, showing superior performance compared to existing MRI reconstruction techniques on various datasets.
elements.sent-2411-372: In future work, we will also extend IGKR-Net to other recovery tasks, such as cardiac MRI.
elements.sent-2411-373: Our method demonstrates superior performance on publicly available datasets, confirming its effectiveness.. \bibliographystyle{cas-model2-names.}
